preamble: The shift from an oral vowel to its nasalized counterpart is a common phonetic process, typically occurring as regressive assimilation when followed by a nasal consonant (VN > 	ilde{V}N), often followed by the loss of the consonant itself.

examples.note: While this example illustrates 'o', the nasalization of 'e' to [ɛ̃] or [ɑ̃] in French follows the same historical trajectory.
Nasalization in Portuguese often targets 'a', but 'e' also undergoes this shift in words like 'vento' [vẽtu].
The process of compensatory lengthening and nasalization seen in 'dā̃t' is also found with other vowels in the Indo-Aryan transition.

sources: Ladefoged, P. (2001). A Course in Phonetics.
